food_subgroup: Yellowfin tuna (Thunnus albacares), raw sashimi-grade fillet, skinless

ontology.foodex2_description: Tuna, yellowfin (Thunnus albacares), raw sashimi-grade fillet

classification.processing_level: unprocessed (raw fresh fish fillet, sashimi grade)
main_ingredients: yellowfin tuna (Thunnus albacares), raw sashimi fillet

serving.natural_language_portion: 5 slices of tuna sashimi weigh approximately 80g. A poke bowl with tuna contains approximately 100–120g raw tuna.

nutrient_source.title: Yellowfin Tuna (raw) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F03010040; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code F03010040 — Yellowfin Tuna, raw fillet.

gi_source: GI not applicable — tuna contains no carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very lean white-fish-like protein (1.0g fat/100g) with high potassium (444mg/100g) and very low sodium (45mg/100g). Omega-3 EPA+DHA content at levels relevant to cardiovascular benefit. Exceptionally high niacin (18.5mg/100g) supports vascular health.
hypertension.guidance: Yellowfin tuna is excellent for patients with hypertension. The omega-3 content supports cardiovascular benefit. However, tuna should be limited to 2 servings per week for some groups (pregnant women, young children) due to moderate mercury content — mercury exposure is a consideration for large predatory fish like tuna.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate. Extraordinary selenium content (108.2mcg/100g) — among the highest of any commonly consumed food. High niacin (18.5mg/100g) and vitamin B6 (0.90mg/100g). Selenium and niacin support insulin signalling pathways.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tuna is highly recommended as a lean protein for patients with type 2 diabetes. Canned tuna in water (drained) is a convenient, affordable alternative to fresh sashimi with nearly equivalent nutritional value. Limit to 2 servings per week due to mercury.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Extraordinary selenium content (108.2mcg/100g) — one of the highest selenium foods available. A single 80g sashimi serving provides 87mcg selenium against a 55–70mcg daily target. Selenium is a critical co-factor for anti-inflammatory antioxidant enzymes. Omega-3 EPA+DHA provide anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tuna is particularly valuable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its exceptional selenium content supporting glutathione peroxidase activity. Omega-3 fatty acids provide direct anti-inflammatory joint benefit. Limit to 2 servings per week due to mercury — supplement with salmon, mackerel, and sardines for additional weekly seafood.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yellowfin tuna (Thunnus albacares, ahi tuna) is a large predatory fish with deep red, lean flesh. Sashimi-grade yellowfin tuna is one of the most nutrient-dense proteins available: 23.4g protein, 1.0g fat, 0 carbohydrate per 100g raw. The selenium content (108.2mcg/100g) is extraordinary — among the highest of any food in the standard diet. Very high niacin (18.5mg/100g — exceeds the entire adult daily niacin requirement in a single 100g serving). Very high vitamin B6 (0.90mg) and magnesium (64mg/100g). Consumed in Singapore as sashimi, poke bowls, tuna nigiri, and canned tuna (in water or oil). The mercury content of large yellowfin tuna is moderate — limiting consumption to 2 servings per week is the standard public health recommendation.
common_user_queries: how often can I eat tuna

confidence_reason: Exact match in HPB Singapore Foodo Database (F03010040).
notes: Values per 100g raw sashimi-grade yellowfin tuna fillet. The extraordinarily high selenium (108.2mcg/100g) reflects the bio-concentration of dietary selenium by tuna throughout the food chain. A 100g serving provides almost 2x the adult daily selenium requirement (55mcg). Mercury note: large yellowfin tuna are at the top of the food chain — mercury bio-concentrates. HPB Singapore recommends limiting large predatory fish (tuna, shark, swordfish) to 2 servings per week for most adults, and limiting further for pregnant women and young children.
